audit_text: evaluate() takes quota_snapshot redundantly. evaluate() accepts it only to populate quota_state_at_decision, which apply_quota_guard() also overwrites. Every caller passes the same snapshot to both functions. Fix: evaluate() returns quota_state_at_decision={}, apply_quota_guard() is the sole owner of that field.
spec_anchor: docs/model-routing-v1-spec.md §App A — frozen RoutingDecision dataclass + §4 quota guard rules
verdict_evidence: evaluate() at routing.py:148-153 takes quota_snapshot:QuotaSnapshot|None as its 4th positional argument. The function uses it exactly once at L201 (quota_state = ... quota_snapshot.quota_state_dict()) and threads the resulting dict into every returned RoutingDecision via quota_state_at_decision=quota_state (L218, L231, L248). No decision branch reads the dict — it is purely a dataclass-population channel. apply_quota_guard at quota.py:123-216 unconditionally recomputes new_state = snapshot.quota_state_dict() (L161) and overwrites quota_state_at_decision on the returned decision in both the no-downgrade branch (L206 replace(...quota_state_at_decision=new_state)) and the downgrade branch (L215 same field in the replace call). Both production callers (routes/routing.py:395-401 and agent/session_assembly.py:289-296) pass the identical snapshot to evaluate() and apply_quota_guard() back-to-back, confirming the audit's redundancy claim. No resolving commit found via git log -S 'quota_snapshot.quota_state_dict()'.
acceptance_criteria: evaluate() in src/bearings/agent/routing.py no longer takes a quota_snapshot parameter; its signature drops the 4th positional argument.
Every RoutingDecision returned by evaluate() carries quota_state_at_decision={} (the dataclass default).
apply_quota_guard() remains the sole writer of quota_state_at_decision; both the no-downgrade and the downgrade branches still set it from snapshot.quota_state_dict() (or {} when snapshot is None).

Both production callers (web/routes/routing.py preview_routing and agent/session_assembly.py) pass only the routing inputs to evaluate() and the snapshot to apply_quota_guard() — no caller passes a snapshot to evaluate().

Existing tests under tests/test_routing.py asserting evaluate() output are updated to construct decisions without a snapshot argument and to assert the empty dict shape; tests asserting end-to-end quota_state_at_decision values move to/remain in tests/test_quota.py.
uv run mypy src/bearings/agent/routing.py src/bearings/agent/quota.py and uv run pytest tests/test_routing.py tests/test_quota.py both pass.
executor_brief: evaluate() takes quota_snapshot only to populate RoutingDecision.quota_state_at_decision, which apply_quota_guard then unconditionally overwrites. Drop the parameter from evaluate(): the function returns decisions with quota_state_at_decision={}, apply_quota_guard becomes the sole owner. Update both production callers (web/routes/routing.py preview, agent/session_assembly.py) to stop threading the snapshot through evaluate(), and migrate tests under tests/test_routing.py + tests/test_quota.py to match.

audit_text: OverrideAggregator.compute() Pass 2 — origin CTE missing window filter. The CTE finds earliest rule-fired messages per session with no time predicate. Works today because override happens at session-creation, but any scenario where a manual message appears inside the window for an out-of-window session would inflate the numerator silently. Coupled-invariant cross-cut to #1 — regression risk on any future session-lifecycle change. Fix: add AND CAST(strftime('%s', created_at) AS INTEGER) >= ? to the origin CTE inner subquery.
code_paths_checked: src/bearings/agent/override_aggregator.py:267-289 (Pass 2 — origin CTE + overrides CTE; origin inner subquery has no window filter)
verdict_evidence: override_aggregator.py:267-289 builds Pass 2 as `WITH origin AS (... SELECT id FROM messages WHERE session_id = m.session_id AND matched_rule_id IS NOT NULL ORDER BY created_at ASC LIMIT 1 ...), overrides AS (SELECT DISTINCT session_id FROM messages WHERE routing_source IN ('manual','manual_override_quota') AND CAST(strftime('%s', created_at) AS INTEGER) >= ?)`. The overrides CTE filters on cutoff_arg; the origin CTE inner subquery (L273-277) does not. As a result the origin CTE selects the all-time earliest rule-fired message per session, even for sessions whose original creation lies outside the 14-day window — and the inner-join with overrides counts any in-window manual message against that out-of-window origin rule, exactly inflating the numerator the audit describes. Pass 1 (L243-254) does include `AND CAST(strftime('%s', created_at) AS INTEGER) >= ?` so denominators correctly count only in-window fires. The asymmetry is the bug. No resolving commit found.
acceptance_criteria: The Pass 2 origin CTE inner subquery in OverrideAggregator.compute() includes `AND CAST(strftime('%s', created_at) AS INTEGER) >= ?` and the corresponding cutoff_arg is bound as an additional placeholder.
After the fix, an override on a session whose creation/origin row lies outside the 14-day window does not contribute to that rule's overridden_count.

A regression test in tests/test_override_aggregator.py constructs a session with an out-of-window origin message and an in-window manual override, runs OverrideAggregator.compute(), and asserts the rule's override_rate is unchanged from the no-override baseline (denominator is 0 for that rule, no row emitted).
uv run pytest tests/test_override_aggregator.py passes.
executor_brief: Pass 2 of OverrideAggregator.compute() builds an `origin` CTE that picks each session's earliest rule-fired message with no time predicate; the `overrides` CTE filters on cutoff but the join with `origin` still admits out-of-window origin sessions, inflating the numerator silently. Add `AND CAST(strftime('%s', created_at) AS INTEGER) >= ?` to the origin CTE inner subquery and bind cutoff_arg one additional time. Cover with a tests/test_override_aggregator.py case where an out-of-window origin session has an in-window manual override and is correctly excluded.

audit_text: _db() and _quota_poller() duplicated across three route files (routes/routing.py, routes/quota.py, routes/usage.py). A routes/_deps.py module fixes this.
spec_anchor: docs/architecture-v1.md §1.1.5 — thin route handlers; CCW-4 utility-module scaffolding
verdict_evidence: src/bearings/web/routes/_deps.py exists at HEAD as a 67-line module defining `_db(request) -> aiosqlite.Connection` (raises 503 when app.state.db_connection missing) and `_quota_poller(request) -> QuotaPoller | None`. Its module docstring explicitly cites the audit (`identified in V1_FEATURE_AUDIT.md feature 3 finding 3`). routes/routing.py:52, routes/quota.py:26, and routes/usage.py:33 all import the helpers from `bearings.web.routes._deps` — no duplicate definitions remain in any of the three files (verified via grep for `def _db` / `def _quota_poller` across src/bearings/web/routes/). git log --oneline --follow src/bearings/web/routes/_deps.py returns a single resolving commit `bda4c513 refactor(web): extract _db/_quota_poller into routes/_deps.py`.
executor_brief: Already fixed in bda4c513 — _db/_quota_poller live in src/bearings/web/routes/_deps.py and are imported by routes/routing.py, routes/quota.py, routes/usage.py. No further work.

audit_text: list_for_tags() is N+1 — one SELECT per tag; could batch with IN(...).
spec_anchor: docs/model-routing-v1-spec.md §3 — evaluation algorithm step 1 (collect rules across tags); docs/architecture-v1.md §4.2 — db/routing.py shape
code_paths_checked: src/bearings/db/routing.py:343-359 (list_for_tags loops over tag_ids and calls list_for_tag once per id)
verdict_evidence: list_for_tags at db/routing.py:343-359 implements the multi-tag fetch as `for tag_id in tag_ids: rules = await list_for_tag(connection, tag_id, enabled_only=enabled_only); out.append((tag_id, rules))` — one SELECT per tag id. With M tag ids and N rules across them, this is O(M) round-trips against the SQLite connection where O(1) (a single `WHERE tag_id IN (?, ?, …) ORDER BY tag_id, priority, id` followed by a Python groupby) is sufficient. The function is on the hot path: it is called by both web/routes/routing.py preview_routing and agent/session_assembly.py per session creation / per preview keystroke. No batching commit exists in HEAD.
acceptance_criteria: list_for_tags() in src/bearings/db/routing.py issues a single SELECT against tag_routing_rules using `WHERE tag_id IN (?, ?, …)` (placeholder count derived from tag_ids length) regardless of len(tag_ids); empty input still short-circuits to [].
Returned shape stays `[(tag_id, [RoutingRule, …]), …]` in the same order as tag_ids; rules within each tag remain ordered by (priority ASC, id ASC).
enabled_only=True still applies the `enabled = 1` clause inside the same single SELECT.

tests/test_routing_db.py covers (a) empty tag_ids, (b) multiple tags with rules, (c) enabled_only=True filtering, (d) preserves caller's tag_ids ordering, and asserts a single SELECT round-trip via a counting connection wrapper or query log.
uv run pytest tests/test_routing_db.py passes.
executor_brief: list_for_tags is N+1 — one SELECT per tag id. Replace the Python loop in src/bearings/db/routing.py with a single `WHERE tag_id IN (?, …)` query plus an in-Python groupby that preserves the caller's tag_ids ordering and the (priority, id) intra-tag ordering. Cover with a tests/test_routing_db.py case asserting a single round-trip across multi-tag input and an empty-input fast path.

audit_text: create_tag_rule catches all IntegrityError as 404 — a future UNIQUE constraint violation would surface as misleading 'tag not found.'
spec_anchor: docs/model-routing-v1-spec.md §9 — POST /api/tags/{id}/routing 404 on missing tag; arch §1.1.5 — error-code mapping discipline
code_paths_checked: src/bearings/db/routing.py:224-300 (create_tag_rule helper — only FK on tag_id is currently in scope)
verdict_evidence: web/routes/routing.py:168-172 catches `aiosqlite.IntegrityError as exc` and unconditionally raises `HTTPException(status_code=404, detail=f'tag {tag_id} not found')`. The current schema's only IntegrityError surface on this path is the FK on `tag_routing_rules.tag_id`, so the 404 mapping is correct today. But any future UNIQUE constraint (e.g. a uniqueness rule on (tag_id, priority) or on rule reasons) would also raise IntegrityError and be misclassified as 'tag not found'. The audit's preferred discipline is to introspect the exception (e.g. inspect the SQLite extended error code or the exception message for FOREIGN KEY vs UNIQUE / CHECK) and map FK to 404, leave UNIQUE/CHECK to surface as 422 or 409. No resolving commit found.
acceptance_criteria: create_tag_rule in web/routes/routing.py distinguishes FK violation on tag_id from other IntegrityError causes — only FK violations map to 404 'tag {tag_id} not found'; UNIQUE/CHECK violations map to 409 (or 422) with a constraint-specific detail string.

The classification uses a stable signal — the SQLite extended error code (sqlite3.SQLITE_CONSTRAINT_FOREIGNKEY, etc.) or, failing that, a tag_existence pre-check before INSERT that raises HTTPException(404) explicitly so the IntegrityError handler is reached only for non-FK constraints.

tests/test_routing_routes.py covers (a) POST against a missing tag_id returns 404, (b) POST that triggers a non-FK IntegrityError (e.g. a deliberately seeded UNIQUE violation, or via a monkeypatched DB layer) returns 409/422 with a non-'tag not found' detail.
uv run pytest tests/test_routing_routes.py passes; uv run mypy src/bearings/web/routes/routing.py passes.
executor_brief: create_tag_rule's POST handler maps every aiosqlite.IntegrityError to a 404 'tag not found' message. That is correct only for the current FK-only failure mode; a future UNIQUE/CHECK constraint will be misclassified. Either pre-check tag existence (raise 404 explicitly, then handle non-FK IntegrityError as 409) or introspect the SQLite extended error code to distinguish FK from UNIQUE/CHECK, and add a regression test that exercises a non-FK IntegrityError path.

audit_text: Phantom validation objects — RoutingRule(id=0, ...) constructed and discarded for validation; calling _validate_rule_fields() directly is cleaner.
verdict_evidence: db/routing.py:153-191 already factors validation into a free function `_validate_rule_fields(*, match_type, match_value, executor_model, advisor_model, advisor_max_uses, effort_level, reason)`. Both dataclass `__post_init__` methods (RoutingRule.__post_init__ at L106-115 and SystemRoutingRule.__post_init__ at L141-150) delegate to it. Despite that, all four CRUD helpers (create_tag_rule L246-260, update_tag_rule L405-419, create_system_rule L562-576, update_system_rule L677-691) still construct a throwaway dataclass instance with id=0 (or the existing rule_id), tag_id/seeded forwarded from existing state, and timestamp fields synthesized — purely to trigger __post_init__ and discard the result. The phantom build inflates allocation pressure and obscures intent: the SAME helpers could replace each phantom with a one-line `_validate_rule_fields(match_type=…, match_value=…, …, reason=reason)` call. No resolving commit found.
acceptance_criteria: create_tag_rule, update_tag_rule, create_system_rule, and update_system_rule in src/bearings/db/routing.py replace their phantom dataclass-constructed-and-discarded blocks with a direct call to _validate_rule_fields(...).
The dataclass __post_init__ methods on RoutingRule and SystemRoutingRule continue to delegate to _validate_rule_fields so the validation alphabet has a single source.

All existing validation behaviour is preserved — invalid match_type, invalid executor/advisor model, negative advisor_max_uses, invalid effort_level, empty reason, and match_value/match_type interaction errors still raise ValueError with the same messages.
tests/test_routing_db.py validation cases (e.g. test_create_tag_rule_rejects_unknown_executor, etc.) still pass without modification.
uv run pytest tests/test_routing_db.py and uv run mypy src/bearings/db/routing.py both pass.
executor_brief: Each of create_tag_rule, update_tag_rule, create_system_rule, and update_system_rule constructs a throwaway RoutingRule/SystemRoutingRule instance solely to trigger __post_init__ validation. _validate_rule_fields is already a public-by-convention helper — replace each phantom block with a direct call (`_validate_rule_fields(match_type=…, …, reason=reason)`) and confirm the existing tests/test_routing_db.py validation suite still passes.

audit_text: quota_state_dict() uses 'or 0.0' — technically correct but 'x if x is not None else 0.0' better expresses intent.
verdict_evidence: quota.py:113-114 builds the dict as `{'overall_used_pct': self.overall_used_pct or 0.0, 'sonnet_used_pct': self.sonnet_used_pct or 0.0}`. The underlying QuotaSnapshot fields are typed `float | None` and the validator at L92-100 explicitly admits 0.0 as a valid percentage. The `x or 0.0` form treats both None and 0.0 as falsy and substitutes 0.0 — which happens to produce the right number for both inputs but loses the readability/intent distinction the audit calls out. The arithmetically-equivalent but semantically explicit `x if x is not None else 0.0` form is preferred. No resolving commit found.
acceptance_criteria: quota_state_dict() in src/bearings/agent/quota.py replaces both `self.overall_used_pct or 0.0` and `self.sonnet_used_pct or 0.0` with `self.overall_used_pct if self.overall_used_pct is not None else 0.0` (and the sonnet equivalent).
Existing tests asserting the dict shape for None inputs and for 0.0 inputs still pass; if no test covers the 0.0 path explicitly, add one to tests/test_quota.py.
uv run pytest tests/test_quota.py and uv run mypy src/bearings/agent/quota.py both pass.
executor_brief: quota_state_dict() at src/bearings/agent/quota.py:113-114 uses `x or 0.0` to coerce a `float | None` percentage to float, which loses the None-vs-0.0 intent distinction. Replace with the explicit `x if x is not None else 0.0` form for both keys; add a tests/test_quota.py case covering the 0.0 input if one is missing.

audit_text: advisor_disabled_reason appears in spec §4 body text but is absent from the frozen Appendix A RoutingDecision shape. Code correctly follows Appendix A.
code_paths_checked: src/bearings/agent/quota.py:123-216 (apply_quota_guard sets source='quota_downgrade' + reason= description; no advisor_disabled_reason)
verdict_evidence: Spec section §4 (lines 233-252) was rewritten in commit `0036f7c1 docs(routing): resolve spec internal inconsistencies` (CCW-5 spec consistency audit). The post-CCW-5 §4 body uses `advisor_model = NULL`, `source = 'quota_downgrade'`, `reason = 'advisor disabled — overall quota ≥ 80%'` — none of which is the dropped advisor_disabled_reason field. Errata E-1 (L610-612) preserves the historical inconsistency for traceability and notes 'Code correctly followed Appendix A throughout.' The audit's underlying claim is accurate but no longer points to a defect — the spec it cited has been corrected. The code (routing.py RoutingDecision dataclass at L64-105 has no such field; quota.py:178-187 conveys advisor-disabled state via source + reason) needs no change.
executor_brief: Superseded — CCW-5 commit 0036f7c1 rewrote §4 to align with Appendix A. Code already follows Appendix A. No further work.

audit_text: No system-rule reorder endpoint in the spec. Frontend works around with N per-rule PATCH calls (confirmed in Svelte component comment). Works but is a spec gap.
verdict_evidence: Spec §9 (line 426) now lists `PATCH /api/routing/system/reorder` with body `{ rule_ids: [id, id, ...] }` mirroring the tag-rule reorder surface. Errata E-3 (L618-621) records that the endpoint was added by CCW-5 (commit 0036f7c1) and explicitly defers implementation to 'feature 3 cleanup'. The spec gap the audit identified no longer exists. The implementation gap (route + frontend wiring not yet in code) is captured separately as discovered finding feature-3-001-extra. No code change is needed against this finding's text per se.
executor_brief: Superseded — CCW-5 commit 0036f7c1 added `PATCH /api/routing/system/reorder` to spec §9. The original spec gap is closed. Implementation gap is tracked under feature-3-001-extra.

audit_text: Discovered during verification: spec §9 (post-CCW-5) mandates `PATCH /api/routing/system/reorder` (added by Errata E-3 with note 'Implementation lands in feature 3 cleanup') but no such route exists in code, and frontend system-rule reorder still uses N per-rule PATCHes.
code_paths_checked: frontend/src/lib/api/routingRules.ts:23-26 (Decided-and-documented surface differences vs spec §9: 'System rules have no documented reorder endpoint; the editor re-stamps priorities by issuing per-rule PATCHes')
verdict_evidence: grep across src/bearings for `system/reorder` and `reorder_system` returns no matches. routes/routing.py defines POST/PATCH/DELETE on /api/routing/system and /api/routing/system/{id} but no /api/routing/system/reorder handler. db/routing.py has `reorder_tag_rules` (L472-514) but no `reorder_system_rules` counterpart. The frontend client at frontend/src/lib/api/routingRules.ts:23-26 documents the workaround ('System rules have no documented reorder endpoint; the editor re-stamps priorities by issuing per-rule PATCHes (reorderSystemRules below) — see the editor docstring for the rationale') and continues to fall back on N PATCH calls. The spec text at docs/model-routing-v1-spec.md:429-431 explicitly defers implementation to 'feature 3 cleanup', so this is a known gap rather than a contradiction.
acceptance_criteria: src/bearings/db/routing.py gains a `reorder_system_rules(connection, ids_in_priority_order)` helper mirroring `reorder_tag_rules`: it validates that every supplied id is an existing system_routing_rules row, re-stamps `priority` columns to (index+1)*10, updates `updated_at` to a single timestamp, and returns the resulting list[SystemRoutingRule] in priority order.

src/bearings/web/routes/routing.py exposes `PATCH /api/routing/system/reorder` accepting `{ rule_ids: [int, …] }` (Pydantic model in src/bearings/web/models/routing.py — match spec body shape verbatim), returning the reordered list[SystemRoutingRuleOut] with operation_id pinned per the SemVer commitment.
ValueError from the helper (unknown id, duplicate id) maps to 422; an empty list returns the unmodified system-rule listing.
frontend/src/lib/api/routingRules.ts replaces the per-rule PATCH workaround with a single call to the new endpoint; the editor docstring is updated accordingly.
tests/test_routing_routes.py covers (a) reorder happy path stamps priority correctly, (b) unknown rule id → 422, (c) duplicate id → 422, (d) empty body → unchanged listing.
tests/test_routing_db.py covers reorder_system_rules's helper-level invariants.
docs/openapi.json regenerated and committed in the same change (per CLAUDE.md OpenAPI export rule).
uv run mypy src tests, uv run pytest, and the frontend gate set (lint, check, test) all pass.
executor_brief: Spec §9 (post-CCW-5) requires `PATCH /api/routing/system/reorder` mirroring tag-rule reorder, but the route, the db helper, and the frontend wiring are all missing — the frontend client docstring still flags it as a known workaround. Land `reorder_system_rules` in src/bearings/db/routing.py, the `PATCH /api/routing/system/reorder` route in src/bearings/web/routes/routing.py with operation_id pinned and a request model in src/bearings/web/models/routing.py, switch frontend/src/lib/api/routingRules.ts to the new endpoint, regenerate docs/openapi.json, and add tests covering reorder happy path, unknown id, duplicate id, and empty body.
